subject: UX & Research
title: The Mars Climate Orbiter
description: A fast breakdown of The Mars Climate Orbiter for builders.

hook.text: NASA lost a $327 million Mars probe because one team used metric and another used imperial units.

buildup.text: Lockheed Martin's software sent thrust data in pounds. NASA's system expected newtons. Nobody caught the mismatch.

discovery.text: The orbiter approached Mars 170 km too low. It burned up in the atmosphere on September 23, 1999.

twist.text: This wasn't just engineering failure. It was an input design failure. The system accepted wrong units silently.

climax.text: Validating units, labeling fields clearly, and confirming critical inputs could have saved the mission.

punchline.text: Label your inputs. Silent acceptance of wrong data is deadly.

quiz.question: What UX lesson does the Mars Climate Orbiter teach?
options: Always label and validate input units to prevent silent errors
Space missions should avoid software entirely
Metric is always better than imperial
